test_text.title: Charlotte's First Day at the Supermarket

audioscript.text: Part 1, you will hear a supervisor in a supermarket talking to a new worker on her first day.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 6.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 1 to 6.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hello, Charlotte.
audioscript.speaker: KAEDEN

audioscript.text: I'm Kaeden, one of the supervisors.
audioscript.speaker: KAEDEN

audioscript.text: Welcome to the team.
audioscript.speaker: KAEDEN

audioscript.text: Hi, Aiden.
audioscript.speaker: CHARLOTTE

audioscript.text: Uh. It's Kaeden.
audioscript.speaker: KAEDEN

audioscript.text: I'm so sorry.
audioscript.speaker: CHARLOTTE

audioscript.text: Oh, don't worry.
audioscript.speaker: KAEDEN

audioscript.text: People often get my name wrong, ha ha.
audioscript.speaker: KAEDEN

audioscript.text: They never know how to spell it.
audioscript.speaker: KAEDEN

audioscript.text: It's K A E D E N, in case you ever need to write it.
audioscript.speaker: KAEDEN

audioscript.text: I'll try and remember.
audioscript.speaker: CHARLOTTE

audioscript.text: So, there are a few practical things you need to sort out this morning.
audioscript.speaker: KAEDEN

audioscript.text: Then I'll show you what you're going to do today.
audioscript.speaker: KAEDEN

audioscript.text: Uh. The email I received said to go to the front desk to show my letter of appointment and pick up my badge.
audioscript.speaker: CHARLOTTE

audioscript.text: You'll need that for the staffroom and other areas of the supermarket where shoppers aren't allowed.
audioscript.speaker: KAEDEN

audioscript.text: So, after you've finished at the front desk, I'll take you to the staffroom.
audioscript.speaker: KAEDEN

audioscript.text: Put your coat and rucksack in one of the lockers there, uh, take whichever one is free.
audioscript.speaker: KAEDEN

audioscript.text: Will I have a key?
audioscript.speaker: CHARLOTTE

audioscript.text: Yes, try not to lose it.
audioscript.speaker: KAEDEN

audioscript.text: At the end of the day, leave it in the door for the next person to use.
audioscript.speaker: KAEDEN

audioscript.text: Will do.
audioscript.speaker: CHARLOTTE

audioscript.text: You also need to go to the HR department to see Tiffany.
audioscript.speaker: KAEDEN

audioscript.text: She's really helpful.
audioscript.speaker: KAEDEN

audioscript.text: I was told to bring my passport with me.
audioscript.speaker: CHARLOTTE

audioscript.text: HR need to take a note of the number in it.
audioscript.speaker: CHARLOTTE

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: KAEDEN

audioscript.text: Or you can show your ID card.
audioscript.speaker: KAEDEN

audioscript.text: I don't have one of those.
audioscript.speaker: CHARLOTTE

audioscript.text: OK, Tiffany will give you a uniform.
audioscript.speaker: KAEDEN

audioscript.text: Uh. They have lots in different sizes, so you just tell her what you need.
audioscript.speaker: KAEDEN

audioscript.text: I won't come with you to HR.
audioscript.speaker: KAEDEN

audioscript.text: I've got to go and sort something else out.
audioscript.speaker: KAEDEN

audioscript.text: Um. Problem with a bread slicer.
audioscript.speaker: KAEDEN

audioscript.text: Is the HR office near the staffroom?
audioscript.speaker: CHARLOTTE

audioscript.text: The staffroom is on the first floor, and HR are a couple of floors above that, on the third floor.
audioscript.speaker: KAEDEN

audioscript.text: There's a staircase outside the staffroom.
audioscript.speaker: KAEDEN

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: CHARLOTTE

audioscript.text: When you've finished with HR, come and find me in the bakery section of the shop.
audioscript.speaker: KAEDEN

audioscript.text: I'm looking forward to getting started.
audioscript.speaker: CHARLOTTE

audioscript.text: Uh. I'll just give you my phone number in case you can't find me.
audioscript.speaker: KAEDEN

audioscript.text: Uh. Have you got your phone there?
audioscript.speaker: KAEDEN

audioscript.text: Uh. Yes, uh OK, ready?
audioscript.speaker: CHARLOTTE

audioscript.text: It's oh four one two double six five nine oh three (0412665903).
audioscript.speaker: KAEDEN

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: CHARLOTTE

audioscript.text: Done.
audioscript.speaker: CHARLOTTE

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 7 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 7 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So, Charlotte, your tasks today are in the bakery section, on the sushi counter, and on the meat and fish counters.
audioscript.speaker: KAEDEN

audioscript.text: The first job is to check sell-by dates on the bread and cakes.
audioscript.speaker: KAEDEN

audioscript.text: If any of the dates are today's, put a new price label on the packaging.
audioscript.speaker: KAEDEN

audioscript.text: What if any of the labels are yesterday's dates or older?
audioscript.speaker: CHARLOTTE

audioscript.text: Do I throw those items away?
audioscript.speaker: CHARLOTTE

audioscript.text: Yes, but that shouldn't happen.
audioscript.speaker: KAEDEN

audioscript.text: We check the stock every day.
audioscript.speaker: KAEDEN

audioscript.text: When something needs a new price label, put a yellow one on the package next to the original price.
audioscript.speaker: KAEDEN

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: CHARLOTTE

audioscript.text: After that, you'll go to the sushi takeaway counter.
audioscript.speaker: KAEDEN

audioscript.text: Will I be preparing boxes of food?
audioscript.speaker: CHARLOTTE

audioscript.text: Uh. For today, you'll just be helping the staff.
audioscript.speaker: KAEDEN

audioscript.text: Yes, of course.
audioscript.speaker: CHARLOTTE

audioscript.text: You'll see lots of flat-cardboard boxes at one end of the counter.
audioscript.speaker: KAEDEN

audioscript.text: Beneath those is where we keep the plastic boxes.
audioscript.speaker: KAEDEN

audioscript.text: We run out of those really quickly, so you should bring more from the storeroom.
audioscript.speaker: KAEDEN

audioscript.text: Is that my only task on the sushi counter?
audioscript.speaker: CHARLOTTE

audioscript.text: No, you also need to clean the area where they prepare the dishes.
audioscript.speaker: KAEDEN

audioscript.text: There are cloths and bottles of spray by the sink.
audioscript.speaker: KAEDEN

audioscript.text: Oh, and please make sure you clean that too.
audioscript.speaker: KAEDEN

audioscript.text: Sure, that's important, isn't it?
audioscript.speaker: CHARLOTTE

audioscript.text: Absolutely, but you mustn't wash up knives.
audioscript.speaker: KAEDEN

audioscript.text: Uh. You have to do some training before you're allowed to touch sharp objects.
audioscript.speaker: KAEDEN

audioscript.text: What should I do, if there are any?
audioscript.speaker: CHARLOTTE

audioscript.text: Ask someone to put them in the dishwasher.
audioscript.speaker: KAEDEN

audioscript.text: OK, thanks.
audioscript.speaker: CHARLOTTE

audioscript.text: I don't want to get anything wrong.
audioscript.speaker: CHARLOTTE

audioscript.text: Don't worry, you'll be fine.
audioscript.speaker: KAEDEN

audioscript.text: And I'll be around to help.
audioscript.speaker: KAEDEN

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: CHARLOTTE

audioscript.text: Uh. Finally the meat and fish counters, you need to clean the area where staff serve customers, including wiping the weighing scales.
audioscript.speaker: KAEDEN

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: CHARLOTTE

audioscript.text: Anything else?
audioscript.speaker: CHARLOTTE

audioscript.text: Um. The fish is laid on ice, but when that starts to melt, you'll need to get more from the cold-room.
audioscript.speaker: KAEDEN

audioscript.text: I know the staff on the food counters wear a hat.
audioscript.speaker: CHARLOTTE

audioscript.text: Will that be the same for me?
audioscript.speaker: CHARLOTTE

audioscript.text: You won't be serving customers directly, so, no, but make sure you put on thermal gloves when you take anything out of the cold-room.
audioscript.speaker: KAEDEN

audioscript.text: The temperature's low enough in there to get frostbite from touching things.
audioscript.speaker: KAEDEN

audioscript.text: Understood.
audioscript.speaker: CHARLOTTE

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

test_text.title: Running Tips and Inspiring Stories from Compton Park Runners Club

audioscript.text: Part 2, you will hear a podcast by a running coach giving advice about taking up running and giving information about her club.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 14.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 14.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: My name's Liz Fuller, and I'm a running coach with Compton Park Runners Club.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ FULLER

audioscript.text: Welcome to my podcast.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ FULLER

audioscript.text: If you're thinking about taking up running, I'm here to help.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ FULLER

audioscript.text: There are many training programs available online, which aim to help people build up to running 5 kilometers.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ FULLER

audioscript.text: Some of them are great, and thousands of people of all ages are taking part in 5 kilometer races across the country as a result.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ FULLER

audioscript.text: People like them because they're easy to follow, and don't push them too hard.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ FULLER

audioscript.text: However, they don't work for everyone, especially if you suffer from something like heart condition or asthma.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ FULLER

audioscript.text: Because they're aimed at people with average fitness and running ability.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ FULLER

audioscript.text: Another thing is that everyone is different, and if you have any specific questions related to your needs.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ FULLER

audioscript.text: There's no one to provide any answers.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ FULLER

audioscript.text: I have a couple of simple tips I always give to new runners.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ FULLER

audioscript.text: I expect you've been told to run very slowly until your fitness increases.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ FULLER

audioscript.text: Well, I find that can prevent progress.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ FULLER

audioscript.text: You should run at a speed that feels comfortable, but time yourself, and try to run a bit faster each time.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ FULLER

audioscript.text: Listening to music can be very helpful.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ FULLER

audioscript.text: It takes your mind off things and helps your body get into a rhythm.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ FULLER

audioscript.text: I'd say that is better than running with a friend.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ FULLER

audioscript.text: Especially as most people are competitive, and that's not what you want when you're just starting.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ FULLER

audioscript.text: I don't think the time of day is especially important.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ FULLER

audioscript.text: Some people are better in the evening, while others are morning people.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ FULLER

audioscript.text: But you need to be consistent, so aim to train regularly.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ FULLER

audioscript.text: Twice a week is enough to begin with.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ FULLER

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the podcast, you have some time to look at questions 15 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 15 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: New members often say to me that they've been put off running, either because they lack confidence, or they don't have time, or they think they dislike running.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ FULLER

audioscript.text: Ceri, for example, joined the club two years ago at the age of 40.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ FULLER

audioscript.text: She'd always enjoyed running at school, but wasn't sure if she'd be able to do it.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ FULLER

audioscript.text: She was worried about being left behind and being the slowest runner, but she says she was made to feel so welcome, she soon forgot all about that.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ FULLER

audioscript.text: James had always hated the idea of running, but a friend encouraged him to come along for a taster session, and he hasn't looked back.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ FULLER

audioscript.text: He never misses a training session,
audioscript.speaker: LIZ FULLER

audioscript.text: despite having a really demanding job.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ FULLER

audioscript.text: Leo was worried about having to commit himself to training sessions every week, and wasn't sure he'd be able to fit training into his busy schedule.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ FULLER

audioscript.text: But after experiencing a lot of stress at work, he came along to us and gave it a go.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ FULLER

audioscript.text: Now he says he feels much more relaxed.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ FULLER

audioscript.text: And he looks forward to his weekly run.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ FULLER

audioscript.text: Mark is quite typical of our new members.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ FULLER

audioscript.text: He's never considered himself to be a sporty person.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ FULLER

audioscript.text: And it was only when he retired that he decided to take up the challenge of trying to run 5 km.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ FULLER

audioscript.text: It took him months to find the courage to contact us.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ FULLER

audioscript.text: But he felt reassured immediately, as there were other people his age who were only just taking up running for the first time.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ FULLER

audioscript.text: My own journey hasn't been easy.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ FULLER

audioscript.text: I did my first marathon when I was 37, after having had two kids.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ FULLER

audioscript.text: My husband had been running marathons for years.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ FULLER

audioscript.text: But I never dreamed I'd be doing one with him.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ FULLER

audioscript.text: I managed to complete it in four hours, but I felt like giving up halfway through.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ FULLER

audioscript.text: It was only the support of the spectators that kept me going.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ FULLER

audioscript.text: I do think signing up for a race of whatever length is motivating, whether it's 5 k or 25 k, because it's good to have something to work towards.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ FULLER

audioscript.text: And it gives you a sense of achievement.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ FULLER

audioscript.text: I did my first 10K after only six months, which was certainly very challenging, and not something I'd necessarily recommend.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ FULLER

audioscript.text: But after you've been training for a few weeks, it's worth putting your name down for a 5 k.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ FULLER

audioscript.text: Some people find they only need a few practice runs before taking part in a race.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ FULLER

audioscript.text: But I'd give yourself a couple of months at least.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ FULLER

audioscript.text: Well, I hope that's given you a really good idea of...
audioscript.speaker: LIZ FULLER

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have 30 seconds to check your answers to part 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Part 2, you will hear a podcast by a running coach giving advice about taking up running and giving information about her club.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 14.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 14.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: My name's Liz Fuller, and I'm a running coach with Compton Park Runners Club.
utterances.speaker: LIZ FULLER

utterances.text: Welcome to my podcast.
utterances.speaker: LIZ FULLER

utterances.text: If you're thinking about taking up running, I'm here to help.
utterances.speaker: LIZ FULLER

utterances.text: There are many training programs available online, which aim to help people build up to running 5 kilometers.
utterances.speaker: LIZ FULLER

utterances.text: Some of them are great, and thousands of people of all ages are taking part in 5 kilometer races across the country as a result.
utterances.speaker: LIZ FULLER

utterances.text: People like them because they're easy to follow, and don't push them too hard.
utterances.speaker: LIZ FULLER

utterances.text: However, they don't work for everyone, especially if you suffer from something like heart condition or asthma.
utterances.speaker: LIZ FULLER

utterances.text: Because they're aimed at people with average fitness and running ability.
utterances.speaker: LIZ FULLER

utterances.text: Another thing is that everyone is different, and if you have any specific questions related to your needs.
utterances.speaker: LIZ FULLER

utterances.text: There's no one to provide any answers.
utterances.speaker: LIZ FULLER

utterances.text: I have a couple of simple tips I always give to new runners.
utterances.speaker: LIZ FULLER

utterances.text: I expect you've been told to run very slowly until your fitness increases.
utterances.speaker: LIZ FULLER

utterances.text: Well, I find that can prevent progress.
utterances.speaker: LIZ FULLER

utterances.text: You should run at a speed that feels comfortable, but time yourself, and try to run a bit faster each time.
utterances.speaker: LIZ FULLER

utterances.text: Listening to music can be very helpful.
utterances.speaker: LIZ FULLER

utterances.text: It takes your mind off things and helps your body get into a rhythm.
utterances.speaker: LIZ FULLER

utterances.text: I'd say that is better than running with a friend.
utterances.speaker: LIZ FULLER

utterances.text: Especially as most people are competitive, and that's not what you want when you're just starting.
utterances.speaker: LIZ FULLER

utterances.text: I don't think the time of day is especially important.
utterances.speaker: LIZ FULLER

utterances.text: Some people are better in the evening, while others are morning people.
utterances.speaker: LIZ FULLER

utterances.text: But you need to be consistent, so aim to train regularly.
utterances.speaker: LIZ FULLER

utterances.text: Twice a week is enough to begin with.
utterances.speaker: LIZ FULLER

utterances.text: Before you hear the rest of the podcast, you have some time to look at questions 15 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen and answer questions 15 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: New members often say to me that they've been put off running, either because they lack confidence, or they don't have time, or they think they dislike running.
utterances.speaker: LIZ FULLER

utterances.text: Ceri, for example, joined the club two years ago at the age of 40.
utterances.speaker: LIZ FULLER

utterances.text: She'd always enjoyed running at school, but wasn't sure if she'd be able to do it.
utterances.speaker: LIZ FULLER

utterances.text: She was worried about being left behind and being the slowest runner, but she says she was made to feel so welcome, she soon forgot all about that.
utterances.speaker: LIZ FULLER

utterances.text: James had always hated the idea of running, but a friend encouraged him to come along for a taster session, and he hasn't looked back.
utterances.speaker: LIZ FULLER

utterances.text: He never misses a training session,
utterances.speaker: LIZ FULLER

utterances.text: despite having a really demanding job.
utterances.speaker: LIZ FULLER

utterances.text: Leo was worried about having to commit himself to training sessions every week, and wasn't sure he'd be able to fit training into his busy schedule.
utterances.speaker: LIZ FULLER

utterances.text: But after experiencing a lot of stress at work, he came along to us and gave it a go.
utterances.speaker: LIZ FULLER

utterances.text: Now he says he feels much more relaxed.
utterances.speaker: LIZ FULLER

utterances.text: And he looks forward to his weekly run.
utterances.speaker: LIZ FULLER

utterances.text: Mark is quite typical of our new members.
utterances.speaker: LIZ FULLER

utterances.text: He's never considered himself to be a sporty person.
utterances.speaker: LIZ FULLER

utterances.text: And it was only when he retired that he decided to take up the challenge of trying to run 5 km.
utterances.speaker: LIZ FULLER

utterances.text: It took him months to find the courage to contact us.
utterances.speaker: LIZ FULLER

utterances.text: But he felt reassured immediately, as there were other people his age who were only just taking up running for the first time.
utterances.speaker: LIZ FULLER

utterances.text: My own journey hasn't been easy.
utterances.speaker: LIZ FULLER

utterances.text: I did my first marathon when I was 37, after having had two kids.
utterances.speaker: LIZ FULLER

utterances.text: My husband had been running marathons for years.
utterances.speaker: LIZ FULLER

utterances.text: But I never dreamed I'd be doing one with him.
utterances.speaker: LIZ FULLER

utterances.text: I managed to complete it in four hours, but I felt like giving up halfway through.
utterances.speaker: LIZ FULLER

utterances.text: It was only the support of the spectators that kept me going.
utterances.speaker: LIZ FULLER

utterances.text: I do think signing up for a race of whatever length is motivating, whether it's 5 k or 25 k, because it's good to have something to work towards.
utterances.speaker: LIZ FULLER

utterances.text: And it gives you a sense of achievement.
utterances.speaker: LIZ FULLER

utterances.text: I did my first 10K after only six months, which was certainly very challenging, and not something I'd necessarily recommend.
utterances.speaker: LIZ FULLER

utterances.text: But after you've been training for a few weeks, it's worth putting your name down for a 5 k.
utterances.speaker: LIZ FULLER

utterances.text: Some people find they only need a few practice runs before taking part in a race.
utterances.speaker: LIZ FULLER

utterances.text: But I'd give yourself a couple of months at least.
utterances.speaker: LIZ FULLER

utterances.text: Well, I hope that's given you a really good idea of...
utterances.speaker: LIZ FULLER

utterances.text: That is the end of part 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have 30 seconds to check your answers to part 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

body.title: Which TWO problems with some training programmes for new runners does Liz mention?
options: There is a risk of serious injury.
They are unsuitable for certain age groups.
They are unsuitable for people with health issues.
It is difficult to stay motivated.
There is a lack of individual support.

body.title: Which TWO tips does Liz recommend for new runners?
options: doing two runs a week
running in the evening
going on runs with a friend
listening to music during runs
running very slowly

text: What reason prevented each of the following members of the Compton Park Runners Club from joining until recently?
textReadable: What reason prevented each of the following members of the Compton Park Runners Club from joining until recently?

items.title: What does Liz say about running her first marathon?
options: It had always been her ambition.
Her husband persuaded her to do it.
She nearly gave up before the end.

items.title: Liz says new runners should sign up for a race
options: every six months.
within a few weeks of taking up running.
after completing several practice runs.

test_text.title: Jane and Kieran's Conversation about Books and Bookshop

audioscript.text: Part 3, you will hear two students called Jane and Kieran talking about books.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 25.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 25.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So, Jane, you'll be off to Denmark soon to do your work placement.
audioscript.speaker: KIERAN

audioscript.text: Yes, I'm really looking forward to it.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: And I've just started packing up all my books to put in storage.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: Well, I hope they don't get spoiled.
audioscript.speaker: KIERAN

audioscript.text: It's OK.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: My grandfather works in a bookshop, and he told me how to pack them.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: Oh, that's helpful.
audioscript.speaker: KIERAN

audioscript.text: He says you have to support the spine, otherwise the paper can come away from the cover.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: Yeah, that's obvious.
audioscript.speaker: KIERAN

audioscript.text: He also told me to pack them flat in the box, not on their side.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: Again because they can bend, and if you leave them like that for, say, a year.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: It's quite hard to get them back to their normal shape.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: Well, it's pretty clear that ruins them, but a lot of people just can't be bothered to protect their books.
audioscript.speaker: KIERAN

audioscript.text: He always says it's such a shame that publishers don't use better quality paper.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: It's the acid in the paper that causes the problem, isn't it?
audioscript.speaker: KIERAN

audioscript.text: Yeah, that's why old books go yellow.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: You know, some of the books my grandfather's given me are like that already.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: I should dump them really, if they're going to deteriorate further, but I'd feel bad.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: They'll always remind me of him, he's quite a collector you know.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: Well, if they're important to you.
audioscript.speaker: KIERAN

audioscript.text: Yeah, I'd regret just throwing them away.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: You know, maybe it's because I was taught to treasure books, but I hate seeing students force open the pages of paperbacks.
audioscript.speaker: KIERAN

audioscript.text: They press so hard they end up breaking the spine.
audioscript.speaker: KIERAN

audioscript.text: I know, but unfortunately paperbacks aren't designed to last a long time, and people know that.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: Hardbacks aren't quite as weak.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: Yeah, they're different, I suppose.
audioscript.speaker: KIERAN

audioscript.text: But I still don't think people value hardbacks like they used to.
audioscript.speaker: KIERAN

audioscript.text: Well, they aren't decorative, are they, like other objects?
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: Plus nowadays people don't keep them out on shelves as much as they used to.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: That's such a pity.
audioscript.speaker: KIERAN

audioscript.text: When I visit someone - if they have, say, a colorful book on a table,
audioscript.speaker: KIERAN

audioscript.text: it's the first thing I'm drawn to.
audioscript.speaker: KIERAN

audioscript.text: I agree.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: And book covers can be a work of art in themselves.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: Some are really eye-catching.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: I've always been taught to handle books carefully.
audioscript.speaker: KIERAN

audioscript.text: If you watch someone take a book off a shelf, well, they usually do it wrong.
audioscript.speaker: KIERAN

audioscript.text: Ah,
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: my grandfather says, you should put your hand right over the top of the book...
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: or if you can't do that, pull the other books on the shelf aside, so that you can hold the whole cover.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: When did you learn all this?
audioscript.speaker: KIERAN

audioscript.text: He watched me pull a heavy book off the shelf when I was small.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: And it fell on the floor and broke apart.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: Oh, dear.
audioscript.speaker: KIERAN

audioscript.text: I can still remember it.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: You know what I really like?
audioscript.speaker: KIERAN

audioscript.text: What?
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: The smell of new books.
audioscript.speaker: KIERAN

audioscript.text: Me too.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: My parents used to laugh at me when I was a kid, because I loved putting books up to my nose.
audioscript.speaker: KIERAN

audioscript.text: Almost as much as reading them.
audioscript.speaker: KIERAN

audioscript.text: New books aren't cheap though, are they?
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: I guess we're lucky we can buy them.
audioscript.speaker: KIERAN

audioscript.text: My grandfather stocks second-hand books as well as new ones.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: And they don't smell quite as good.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the discussion, you have some time to look at questions 26 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 26 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: I'd love to have a bookshop like your grandfather.
audioscript.speaker: KIERAN

audioscript.text: What's it like?
audioscript.speaker: KIERAN

audioscript.text: Well, it's quite big.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: It's got two floors and an attic.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: And he stocks all kinds of books, really.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: I guess he treasures things like first editions and other rare books.
audioscript.speaker: KIERAN

audioscript.text: Yeah, you might think he'd keep those in the attic or somewhere.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: So they'd be hidden.
audioscript.speaker: KIERAN

audioscript.text: Yeah, but he likes people to know that he has them.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: So he puts them out in the shop, but makes sure you need a ladder to get them.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: Right, that would prevent any thefts.
audioscript.speaker: KIERAN

audioscript.text: Uh-huh.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: Does he stock books for children?
audioscript.speaker: KIERAN

audioscript.text: He does, he particularly likes to encourage kids to read.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: He always says that he used to sit under the stairs as a child with a pile of books and read them all.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: Is that where he keeps them, then?
audioscript.speaker: KIERAN

audioscript.text: Ah not exactly.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: He's got a dedicated area on the ground floor with cushions, so that parents can enter with their toddlers, go there, and spend some time reading to them.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: Oh, Cool.
audioscript.speaker: KIERAN

audioscript.text: And then there's a place for pushchairs by the front door, and a café if anyone needs refreshments.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: That's good to know.
audioscript.speaker: KIERAN

audioscript.text: As I said, it's a big shop.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: And there's a storage area out the back as well.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: Oh, what does he keep there?
audioscript.speaker: KIERAN

audioscript.text: Books he wants to throw away?
audioscript.speaker: KIERAN

audioscript.text: He hardly ever throws anything away.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: He just leaves unwanted books by the front door for customers to take.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: Well, that's very nice.
audioscript.speaker: KIERAN

audioscript.text: Yeah - and books, people or institutions have requested, they all go at the far end.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: Oh, he thinks it's best to keep these out of the main shopping area as they're boxed and new.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: Did you get your course books from him?
audioscript.speaker: KIERAN

audioscript.text: Naturally.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: He stocks books for a lot of the colleges.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: He used to keep these books on the first floor.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: But now there's a new university in my hometown.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: He's moved them downstairs to attract the students.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: They're actually part of the coffee shop on low shelves all around it.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: Pretty central then, you'll have to take me there sometime.
audioscript.speaker: KIERAN

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have 30 seconds to check your answers to part 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.speaker: JANE

utterances.text: Did you get your course books from him?
utterances.speaker: KIERAN

utterances.text: Naturally.
utterances.speaker: JANE

utterances.text: He stocks books for a lot of the colleges.
utterances.speaker: JANE

utterances.text: He used to keep these books on the first floor.
utterances.speaker: JANE

utterances.text: But now there's a new university in my hometown.
utterances.speaker: JANE

utterances.text: He's moved them downstairs to attract the students.
utterances.speaker: JANE

utterances.text: They're actually part of the coffee shop on low shelves all around it.
utterances.speaker: JANE

utterances.text: Pretty central then, you'll have to take me there sometime.
utterances.speaker: KIERAN

utterances.text: That is the end of part 3.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have 30 seconds to check your answers to part 3.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: Kieran thinks the packing advice given by Jane's grandfather is
options: common sense.
hard to follow.
over-protective.

items.title: How does Jane feel about the books her grandfather has given her?
options: They are not worth keeping.
They should go to a collector.
They have sentimental value for her.

items.title: Jane and Kieran agree that hardback books should be
options: put out on display.
given as gifts to visitors.
more attractively designed.

items.title: While talking about taking a book from a shelf, Jane
options: describes the mistakes other people make doing it.
reflects on a significant childhood experience.
explains why some books are easier to remove than others.

items.title: What do Jane and Kieran suggest about new books?
options: Their parents liked buying them as presents.
They would like to buy more of them.
Not everyone can afford them.

text: Where does Jane's grandfather keep each of the following types of books in his shop?
textReadable: Where does Jane's grandfather keep each of the following types of books in his shop?

list: at the back of the shop
body.title: Types of books

test_text.title: Reforestation: Challenges, Best Practices, and Community Involvement

audioscript.text: Part 4, you will hear part of an environmental studies lecture on tree planting.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Tree planting now dominates political and popular agendas, and is often presented as an easy answer to the climate crisis, as well as a way for business corporations to offset their carbon emissions.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But unfortunately, tree planting isn't as straightforward as some people think.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: When the wrong trees are planted in the wrong place, it can do considerably more damage than good.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Failing to help either people or the environment.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Reforestation projects are currently being undertaken on a huge scale in many countries, and it's crucial that the right trees are selected.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: A mix of species should always be planted, typical of the local natural forest ecosystem, and including rare and endangered species in order to create a rich ecosystem.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's important to avoid non-native species that could become invasive.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Invasive species are a significant contributor to the current global biodiversity crisis.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And are often in competition with native species, and may threaten their long-term survival.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Restoring biodiversity that will maximize carbon capture is key when reforesting an area, but ideally any reforestation project should have several goals.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: These could include selecting trees that can contribute to wildlife conservation, improve the availability of food for the local community, and maintain the stability of soil systems.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Meeting as many of these goals as possible, whilst doing no harm to local communities, native ecosystems and vulnerable species, is the sign of a highly successful tree-planting scheme.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: To ensure the survival and resilience of a planted forest, it's vital to use tree seeds with appropriate levels of genetic diversity.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The amount of genetic variation found within a species is essential for their survival.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Using seeds with low genetic diversity generally lowers the resilience of restored forests, which can make them vulnerable to disease, and unable to adapt to climate change.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Choosing the right location for reforestation projects is as important as choosing the right trees.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Ultimately, the best area for planting trees would be formerly forested areas that are in poor condition.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's better to avoid non-forested landscapes, such as natural grasslands, savannas or wetlands.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As these ecosystems already contribute greatly to capturing carbon, it would also be advantageous to choose an area where trees could provide other benefits.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Such as recreational spaces, reforesting areas which are currently exploited for agriculture should be avoided.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As this often leads to other areas being deforested.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Large scale reforestation projects require careful planning.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Making the right decisions about where to plant trees depends on having the right information.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Having detailed and up-to-date maps, identifying high-priority areas for intervention is essential.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Drone technology is a useful tool in helping to prioritize and monitor areas of degraded forest for restoration, in Brazil, it's being used to identify and quantify how parts of the Amazon are being devastated by human activities.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Such as rearing cattle and illegal logging.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: A good example of where the right trees were picked to achieve a restored forest is in Lampang province in northern Thailand.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: A previously forested site which had been degraded through mining was reforested by a cement company together with Chiang Mai University.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: After spreading 60 cm of top soil, they planted 14 different native tree species, which included several species of fig.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Figs are a keystone species, because of the critical role they play in maintaining wildlife populations.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They are central to tropical reforestation projects.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As they accelerate the speed of the recovery process by attracting animals and birds, which act as natural seed dispersers.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This helps to promote diversity through the healthy regrowth of a wide range of plant species.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Unlike the majority of fruit trees, figs bear fruit all year round, providing a reliable food source for many species.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: At this site for example, after only three rainy seasons, monkeys started visiting to eat the fig fruits, naturally dispersing seeds through defecation.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Reforestation projects should always aim to make sure that local communities are consulted and involved in the decision making process.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The restoration of mangrove forests in Madagascar is an example of a project which has succeeded in creating real benefits for the community.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Destruction of the mangrove forests had a terrible impact on plant and animal life, and also badly affected the fishing industry.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Which was a major source of employment for local people living in coastal areas, the reforestation project involved hiring local people.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: To plant and care for the new mangrove trees.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Millions of mangrove trees have now been planted, which has resulted in the return of a healthy aquatic ecosystem.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The mangroves also act as a defense against the increased threat of flooding caused by climate change.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: What's more, the local economy is more stable.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And thousands more Madagascans are now able to send their children to school.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: One other important point to consider.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: That is the end of the listening test.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: In the IELTS test, you would now have 10 minutes to transfer your answers to the listening answer sheet.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER
